Calculus.Limits & Continuity:
A limit describes what value a function approaches as its input gets arbitrarily close to some point — not necessarily what the function equals there. We write lim(x→a) f(x) = L to mean f(x) gets as close to L as we want by taking x close enough to a.

Limits are one-sided (from the left or right) or two-sided. The two-sided limit exists only when both one-sided limits agree. A function is continuous at a point a if three things hold: f(a) is defined, the limit exists, and the limit equals f(a).

Key results: lim(x→0) sin(x)/x = 1. For stat theory, limits underpin convergence of estimators and the definition of the likelihood derivative.
Calculus.Derivatives:
The derivative f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h is the instantaneous rate of change. Core rules: power rule, product rule, quotient rule, chain rule. Key: d/dx(e^x)=e^x, d/dx(ln x)=1/x.

In statistics, derivatives find MLEs — differentiate the log-likelihood and set to zero. Fisher information and the Cramer-Rao bound rely on log-likelihood derivatives.
Calculus.Integration:
Integration reverses differentiation and measures accumulated area. The Fundamental Theorem: if F'(x)=f(x) then integral from a to b of f(x)dx = F(b)-F(a).

Key techniques: u-substitution, integration by parts (integral u dv = uv - integral v du), partial fractions.

In probability, every expectation, variance, and MGF is an integral. The PDF integrates to 1; E[X] = integral x f(x) dx.
Calculus.Multivariable Calculus:
Partial derivatives treat all variables but one as constant. The gradient ∇f points in the direction of steepest ascent. Double and triple integrals extend integration to regions in 2D and 3D.

The Jacobian determinant handles change-of-variables in multiple integrals. In statistics this appears in distribution transformations.

The multivariate normal PDF requires matrix inverses and determinants; computing probabilities requires multidimensional integration.
Calculus.Series & Convergence:
An infinite series converges if partial sums approach a finite limit. Key tests: ratio test, p-series test, comparison test.

Taylor series: f(x) = sum f^(n)(a)/n! (x-a)^n. Most important: e^x = sum x^n/n!.

In probability, the MGF M_X(t) = E[e^(tX)] is a power series in t. Its k-th derivative at t=0 gives E[X^k]. The CLT proof relies on Taylor expansion of MGFs.

Linear Algebra.Vectors & Matrices:
A vector is an ordered list of numbers. The dot product u·v = sum u_i v_i = |u||v|cos(θ). Orthogonal means dot product zero.

Matrix multiplication: (AB)_{ij} = row i of A dotted with column j of B. Not commutative. Transpose A^T flips rows and columns. Symmetric means A = A^T.

The covariance matrix is always symmetric positive semi-definite. The regression model Y = Xβ + ε requires matrix algebra throughout.
Linear Algebra.Systems of Equations:
A system Ax = b can have zero, one, or infinitely many solutions. Row reduction finds solutions via REF or RREF.

Rank = number of linearly independent rows/columns. Full column rank means at most one solution. The null space is {x: Ax=0}; its dimension is n - rank(A).

Least squares solves the normal equations A^T A x = A^T b. Full column rank of the design matrix X is required for a unique regression solution — it fails under perfect multicollinearity.
Linear Algebra.Determinants & Inverses:
det(A) for 2x2 [[a,b],[c,d]] is ad-bc. A is invertible iff det(A) ≠ 0. Key: det(AB) = det(A)det(B), det(A^T) = det(A).

The multivariate normal PDF contains det(Σ) in the normalizing constant. Σ must be positive definite (det > 0) for the distribution to be non-degenerate.
Linear Algebra.Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors:
Av = λv for nonzero v means λ is an eigenvalue. Find eigenvalues by solving det(A - λI) = 0. The spectral theorem: real symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors (A = QDQ^T).

Positive definite means all eigenvalues > 0. The covariance matrix Σ is positive semi-definite. Its eigendecomposition gives PCA directions. Eigenvalues of Σ appear in quadratic forms X^T Σ^{-1} X.
Linear Algebra.Projections & Least Squares:
The projection of b onto col(A) is Hb where H = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T. H is symmetric and idempotent (H^2 = H).

The residual b - Hb is orthogonal to col(A). Least squares minimizes ||b - Ax||^2 and gives the normal equations.

In regression, fitted values Y-hat = HY are the projection of Y onto col(X). Residuals e = Y - Y-hat satisfy X^T e = 0. The SS decomposition SST = SSR + SSE follows from the Pythagorean theorem applied to this orthogonal decomposition.

Probability.Axioms & Counting:
Kolmogorov axioms: (1) P(A) ≥ 0, (2) P(Ω) = 1, (3) countable additivity for disjoint events. Everything follows.

Counting: multiplication rule, permutations P(n,k) = n!/(n-k)!, combinations C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!).

Inclusion-exclusion: P(A∪B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B).
Probability.Conditional Probability & Bayes:
P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B). Multiplication rule: P(A∩B) = P(A|B)P(B). Law of total probability: P(A) = sum P(A|B_i)P(B_i).

Bayes' theorem: P(B|A) = P(A|B)P(B)/P(A). The posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior. This is the foundation of Bayesian statistics.

Independence: P(A∩B) = P(A)P(B). Independent events are not mutually exclusive (unless one has probability 0).
Probability.Random Variables & Distributions:
Discrete RVs: PMF P(X=k). Continuous RVs: PDF f(x) where P(a≤X≤b) = integral f(x)dx. CDF: F(x) = P(X≤x).

Key distributions: Poisson(λ), Binomial(n,p), Exponential(λ) with memoryless property, Normal(μ,σ^2), Gamma(α,β), Beta(α,β).

Exponential families f(x;θ) = h(x)exp(η(θ)T(x) - A(θ)) include most common distributions and have elegant sufficiency properties.
Probability.Expectation & Variance:
E[X] = sum k P(X=k) or integral x f(x) dx. LOTUS: E[g(X)] = integral g(x)f(x)dx — no need to find the distribution of g(X).

Linearity: E[aX+bY] = aE[X]+bE[Y] always, regardless of dependence.

Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. For independent X,Y: Var(aX+bY) = a^2 Var(X) + b^2 Var(Y).

MGF: M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]. The k-th derivative at 0 gives E[X^k]. For independent X,Y: M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)M_Y(t).
Probability.Joint Distributions:
Joint PDF f(x,y). Marginals: f_X(x) = integral f(x,y) dy. Conditional: f_{Y|X}(y|x) = f(x,y)/f_X(x).

Independence iff f(x,y) = f_X(x)f_Y(y). Zero covariance does not imply independence (except for jointly normal variables).

Jacobian for transformations: f_{U,V}(u,v) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)|det(J)|^{-1}. The multivariate normal has normal marginals and conditionals.
Probability.Limit Theorems:
WLLN: P(|X-bar - μ| > ε) → 0 for any ε > 0 (convergence in probability). SLLN: X-bar → μ almost surely (stronger).

CLT: sqrt(n)(X-bar - μ)/σ →^d N(0,1) for i.i.d. X_i with finite variance. Justifies normal approximations universally.

Delta method: if sqrt(n)(X-bar-μ) →^d N(0,σ^2) and g is differentiable, then sqrt(n)(g(X-bar)-g(μ)) →^d N(0,[g'(μ)]^2 σ^2). Used constantly for asymptotic inference.

Stat Theory.Point Estimation:
Estimator T(X_1,...,X_n) estimates unknown θ. MLE maximizes l(θ) = sum log f(x_i;θ). Method of moments sets sample moments equal to population moments.

Unbiased: E[T] = θ. MSE = Var(T) + Bias^2. Cramer-Rao: Var(T) ≥ 1/I(θ) for unbiased T, where I(θ) is Fisher information.

MLEs are consistent, asymptotically normal (sqrt(n)(θ-hat-θ) →^d N(0,1/I(θ))), and asymptotically efficient.
Stat Theory.Sufficient Statistics:
T(X) is sufficient for θ if the conditional distribution of X|T(X) does not depend on θ — T captures all information about θ.

Factorization theorem: T(X) sufficient iff f(x;θ) = g(T(x),θ)h(x). Complete sufficient statistic: E[g(T)]=0 for all θ implies g(T)=0 a.s.

Lehmann-Scheffe: unbiased estimator that is a function of a complete sufficient statistic is the UMVUE. Exponential families have natural complete sufficient statistics.
Stat Theory.Hypothesis Testing:
Test H_0 vs H_1. Type I error: P(reject H_0 | H_0 true) = α. Type II error: P(fail to reject | H_1 true) = β. Power = 1-β.

Neyman-Pearson: for simple vs simple, reject when L(θ_1)/L(θ_0) > k. This is the most powerful test of size α.

For composite H_0, LRT rejects when 2log(L(θ-hat)/L(θ_0)) > c. By Wilks' theorem this is asymptotically chi-squared(r) under H_0.

p-value: probability under H_0 of a test statistic at least as extreme as observed. Not the probability H_0 is true.
Stat Theory.Confidence Intervals:
A 100(1-α)% CI (L(X), U(X)) satisfies P(L(X) ≤ θ ≤ U(X)) = 1-α. The probability is over the random endpoints, not over θ.

Method: find pivot Q(X,θ) with distribution free of θ. Invert P(q_{α/2} ≤ Q ≤ q_{1-α/2}) = 1-α to isolate θ.

Normal with known σ: X-bar ± z_{α/2}·σ/sqrt(n). Unknown σ: X-bar ± t_{n-1,α/2}·S/sqrt(n).

Duality: the 95% CI is exactly the set of θ_0 not rejected at 5% by a two-sided test.
